risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "randomization was stratified according to the body regions of the primary surgery... Subsequently, randomization was performed... by using the sealed envelope method." Comment: The study describes a stratified randomization process with allocation by sealed envelopes, which indicates a deliberate random allocation sequence rather than a predictable or non-random rule. Although the exact sequence generation mechanism is not fully specified, the reported procedure is sufficient to support a low risk judgement for random sequence generation.

Quote: "randomization was performed when the patient was in the anesthetic room immediately before surgery by using the sealed envelope method." Comment: Although the paper does not explicitly say "opaque" or "sequentially numbered," it reports a sealed-envelope allocation process carried out immediately before surgery, which is the only stated mechanism for assignment and indicates an attempt to prevent foreknowledge of the next allocation.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The surgical team in theatre were aware of group allocation, but only following the induction of anesthesia." Comment: Participants were not blinded to the intervention they received, and the surgeons/personnel delivering treatment knew allocation during the procedure. The main outcomes included wound healing time, pain (VAS), blood loss, and length of hospital stay, all of which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of group assignment and by unblinded care/delivery, so the study is at high risk of performance bias overall.

Quote: "clinicians undertaking the follow-up of postoperative wounds were fully blinded to the group assignments." Comment: The outcome assessor for wound healing was the follow-up clinician, and the paper explicitly states that these clinicians were fully blinded, so knowledge of allocation was unlikely to influence assessment of wound healing. The main outcomes assessed here (wound healing time, blood loss, length of stay, and VAS pain recorded during follow-up) were measured by blinded follow-up clinicians or were objective/record-based outcomes, making detection bias unlikely.

Quote: "No patients were lost during the follow-up period." / "All the patients enrolled were followed up for at least 4 weeks following surgery" Comment: Follow-up appears essentially complete with no reported losses to follow-up or missing outcome data, so attrition is unlikely to have biased the results.
